narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early academic deciles, LSAT score of 23 (well below the average of 36.7), lower-middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time like most applicants (92.7% do). It calculated a 47.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize admitting those predicted to pass and succeed in practice. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA was the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar passage because it's competitive with what succeeds for others. However, your Year 3 school decile of 1—much lower than the average 5.53—had the most powerful counteracting effect, strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure based on similar past students. Your family income bracket also helped your case somewhat, nudging the odds toward passing compared to higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. These top three elements—your GPA helping, Year 3 decile hurting most, and income mildly aiding—together tipped the scales despite your full-time commitment. Your low LSAT and early decile of 1 (versus average 5.71) added concerns but weren't the dominant drivers here. This outcome reflects how the model weighs patterns from thousands of students like you, where weaker later performance often predicts bar struggles more than other traits. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience from your lower-middle background. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value determined applicants like yourself.
